section.title: Solomon's Wedding Procession
section.sectionRationale: A new voice cries out as a splendid procession rises from the wilderness in a column of perfumed smoke: it is King Solomon’s carriage, guarded by sixty mighty men, coming on his wedding day. The scene lifts love from the private world of the lovers into public, royal celebration, crowned by the king in the day of his heart’s rejoicing.
section.thesis: Love arrives in public splendor: the king comes in a guarded, perfumed procession on his wedding day, and the daughters of Zion are called to come out and gaze at the crowned bridegroom in the day of his heart’s rejoicing.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the question and the sight, who is this coming up from the wilderness, revealed as Solomon’s guarded carriage; and the call to behold the crowned king on his wedding day, the day of his heart’s rejoicing.

verses.text: Who is this coming up from the wilderness like a column of smoke, scented with myrrh and frankincense from all the spices of the merchant?
Behold, it is Solomon’s carriage, escorted by sixty of the mightiest men of Israel.
All are skilled with the sword, experienced in warfare. Each has his sword at his side prepared for the terror of the night.
King Solomon has made his carriage out of the timber of Lebanon.
He has made its posts of silver, its base of gold, its seat of purple fabric. Its interior is inlaid with {{ahavah|love}} by the daughters of Jerusalem.

units.label: Behold the crowned king
units.note: the day of his heart's rejoicing

verses.text: Come out, O daughters of Zion, and gaze at King Solomon, wearing the crown with which his mother crowned him on the day of his {{chatunnah|wedding}}, the day of his heart’s rejoicing.

ahavah.gloss: Love. The very interior of the king’s carriage is said to be inlaid or fitted out with love; the whole procession is framed and furnished by love, ahavah, the word that crowns the Song.

chatunnah.gloss: Wedding, the day of espousal. This is the only appearance of this word in the Old Testament, marking the joyful wedding day of the king, called the day of his heart’s rejoicing.

basic.context: A watcher cries out as a grand procession rises from the wilderness in a pillar of fragrant smoke. It is King Solomon’s royal carriage, guarded by sixty of Israel’s best soldiers, built of the finest timber and gold, its inside fitted out with love. The daughters of Zion are called to come and see the king wearing his wedding crown on the happiest day of his life. Love here becomes public, royal, and celebrated.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a wedding poem, a set piece of royal splendor. On the plain level it lifts the lovers’ private joy into a public celebration worthy of a king’s wedding. Within the whole Bible, the picture of a crowned king coming to his wedding, guarded and glorious, points forward to Christ the King and His wedding day. The literal splendor opens onto the gospel.
basic.originalLanguages: The carriage is inlaid with ahavah, love, the word that runs through the Song. And the day is called the king’s chatunnah, his wedding, a word found only here in the Old Testament, matched with the phrase the day of his heart’s rejoicing. The whole scene is love made public and crowned with joy.
basic.typology: A crowned king coming to his wedding, guarded against every terror of the night, pictures Christ the King who comes for His bride. The joy of the wedding day anticipates the marriage supper of the Lamb.
basic.christ: Solomon, crowned and rejoicing on his wedding day, is a shadow of the greater King. Christ is the Bridegroom who comes for His church, crowned now with glory, whose heart rejoices over His people, and whose wedding day the whole story is moving toward. The daughters of Zion are called to come and gaze; so are we.
basic.god: God delights in the joy of a wedding and is not aloof from human celebration. More than that, He is preparing a wedding of His own, the union of Christ and His people, over which His heart rejoices as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 62:5, as a bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so your God will rejoice over you. Revelation 19:7, the marriage of the Lamb has come. Psalm 45, a royal wedding psalm for the king.
basic.oneStory: The wedding procession lifts love into public splendor and joy. It points beyond Solomon to Christ the King, whose wedding day the Bible is moving toward, when the Bridegroom will rejoice over His bride forever.

context.literary: A distinct wedding poem, opening with the arresting question, who is this coming up from the wilderness, a question repeated of the bride in 8:5. It moves from the sight of the guarded, glorious carriage to the climactic call to behold the crowned king on his wedding day.
context.historical: Royal weddings in the ancient world were public spectacles of splendor: perfumed processions, armed guards against bandits and the terror of the night, and costly materials, timber of Lebanon, silver, gold, and purple. The crowning by the king’s mother reflects the honored role of the queen mother.
context.canonical: The royal wedding imagery joins Psalm 45 and the prophetic vision of God as bridegroom, and it reaches forward to the New Testament portrait of Christ the King and Bridegroom, culminating in the marriage supper of the Lamb in Revelation 19.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To celebrate love made public and royal on the wedding day, portraying the king’s glorious, guarded procession and calling the community to rejoice with the bridegroom in the day of his heart’s gladness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is descriptive, a portrait of a wedding procession, rather than a set of commands. Its abiding truth is the goodness of love celebrated publicly and the joy proper to a wedding, which Scripture takes up as a picture of Christ and His bride.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: The scene is a royal wedding procession, the public celebration of a marriage; it fits most naturally the joy of a real wedding, which Scripture honors, Psalm 45 and Genesis 2.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: The church has long seen in the crowned king coming to his wedding a picture of Christ the King and His bride, in step with the Bible’s bridegroom imagery, Isaiah 62 and Revelation 19.

views.label: Both, the literal wedding as a God-given sign
views.case: The fullest reading keeps both: the poem truly celebrates a royal wedding, and because marriage is a God-given sign of Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, the crowned bridegroom rightly points to the greater King and His wedding day.

debates.bearing: Holding both keeps the wedding joy earthy and real while letting it point to the marriage of the Lamb, so the passage neither becomes bare pageantry nor loses its gospel horizon.

study.originalLanguages: Two words crown the scene. The carriage is inlaid with ahavah, love, so that the very furnishing of the king’s procession is love, the theme that runs through the whole Song. And the day is named his chatunnah, his wedding, a word that appears only here in the Old Testament, set beside the phrase the day of his heart’s rejoicing. Love here is not hidden but paraded, crowned, and celebrated, a fitting shadow of the King whose wedding day is the joy of the whole redeemed creation.

typology.type: The crowned king coming to his wedding day
typology.fulfillment: Christ the King and Bridegroom, crowned with glory, coming for His bride, Hebrews 2:9 and Revelation 19:7.

typology.type: The guarded procession against the terror of the night
typology.fulfillment: Christ who keeps and protects His people through every darkness on the way to the wedding, John 10:28 to 29.

typology.type: The day of his heart’s rejoicing
typology.fulfillment: The joy of God the Bridegroom over His people, Isaiah 62:5, consummated at the marriage supper of the Lamb.

study.christ: Solomon crowned and rejoicing on his wedding day is a shadow of the greater King. Christ is the Bridegroom who comes for His church, now crowned with glory and honor, whose heart rejoices over His people as a bridegroom over his bride. The whole biblical story moves toward His wedding day, the marriage supper of the Lamb. The daughters of Zion are called to come out and gaze at the king; the church is called to fix her eyes on Christ and rejoice that His joy is to have her as His own.
study.god: God is shown to delight in the joy of a wedding, entering into human celebration rather than standing aloof from it. More than this, He is Himself a rejoicing Bridegroom: the same heart that rejoices over Solomon’s wedding day rejoices over the union of Christ and His people, a joy the prophets promise and Revelation consummates.

crossReferences.note: A royal wedding psalm celebrating the king, applied to Christ in Hebrews 1.
As a bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so shall your God rejoice over you.
Let us rejoice, for the marriage of the Lamb has come and His bride has made herself ready.
The same question, who is this coming up from the wilderness, is later asked of the bride leaning on her beloved.

study.oneStory: The wedding procession lifts love out of the private world of the lovers into public, royal splendor and joy. Within the whole story it points beyond Solomon to Christ the King and Bridegroom, whose wedding day the Bible is moving toward, when the King will be crowned in the sight of His people and His heart will rejoice over His bride forever.

quiz.q: What is coming up from the wilderness in 3:6 to 7?

options.text: King Solomon’s wedding procession, a perfumed, guarded royal carriage.
options.note: The pillar of fragrant smoke reveals the king’s glorious wedding procession.

options.text: An invading army bent on destruction.
options.note: Armed guards do surround it, but the scene is a wedding procession, not an army bent on destruction.
The procession is indeed perfumed with the merchant’s fragrant powders, but it is King Solomon’s wedding carriage, not a caravan of ordinary merchants.
It is a wedding, the day of the king’s heart’s rejoicing, not a funeral.

quiz.q: Why are the sixty mighty men present in 3:7 to 8?

options.text: To guard the king’s procession, swords ready against the terror of the night.
options.note: They are an armed escort protecting the wedding journey.
They are indeed armed warriors expert in war, but they guard the king’s procession against the terror of the night; they do not attack the bride’s family.
They are warriors escorting the king, not traders.

options.text: To compete for the bride themselves.
options.note: They do surround the bridal carriage, but as protectors of the king, not as rivals competing for the bride.

quiz.q: What are the daughters of Zion called to do in 3:11?

options.text: Come out and gaze at King Solomon, crowned on the day of his wedding and heart’s rejoicing.
options.note: The climax is a call to behold the crowned, rejoicing bridegroom.

options.text: Stay hidden and take no notice of the king.
options.note: They are summoned to come out and gaze at him.
They are indeed summoned to respond to the king, but with rejoicing on his wedding day, not mourning a departure.
The crown is truly in view, but it is his mother who crowned him; the daughters are called to come out and behold him crowned, not to crown him.

quiz.q: How does the crowned bridegroom point to Christ?

options.text: He foreshadows Christ the King and Bridegroom, crowned with glory, coming for His bride, whose wedding day the whole story awaits.
options.note: The scene points to the marriage supper of the Lamb.

options.text: He teaches that only kings can be loved.
options.note: The scene does exalt a king, but the point is the royal wedding as a sign of Christ, not that only kings can be loved.

options.text: He shows that God takes no delight in weddings.
options.note: The opposite: God rejoices as a bridegroom over his bride, Isaiah 62.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: The scene truly depicts a real royal wedding, yet Scripture reads the crowned bridegroom as a picture of Christ and His church, so it does point beyond itself.

questions: The wedding lifts private love into public, joyful celebration. Where might God be inviting you to rejoice openly in His goodness rather than keeping your joy hidden?
The king’s carriage is guarded against the terror of the night. How does it comfort you that Christ guards His people through every darkness on the way to the wedding?
The daughters of Zion are called to come out and gaze at the crowned king. What would it look like this week to fix your gaze on Christ the King and rejoice?
It is called the day of the king’s heart’s rejoicing. Have you considered that Christ’s joy is to have you as His own, and how might that reshape how you see yourself?
The whole story moves toward the marriage supper of the Lamb. How does living in hope of that wedding day change the way you face this week?

translationNotes.note: Who is this coming up from the wilderness opens the wedding scene and is echoed of the bride in 8:5.
Solomon’s carriage renders a word for a portable couch or litter on which servants carried the king.
Wedding here translates a Hebrew word found only in this verse; the day of his heart’s rejoicing marks it as a day of great gladness.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 3:6-11, chapter 3. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. The source em dash at the close of 3:11 (on the day of his wedding) was converted to a comma; no dashes remain.
